Paper ID: P01
Title: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in Blockchain Online Social Media
Authors: Davide Mancino, Barbara Guidi, Andrea Michienzi, Marco Viviani
Year: 2025
Published in: IEEE Access
Domain: blockchain
Keywords: Blockchain online social media, content quality, decentralized online social networks, natural language processing, reward systems, social media, user-generated content.

Section: Conclusion
Paper: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in Blockchain Online Social Media

CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK FUTURE WORK
Current reward systems in Blockchain-based Online Social
Media (BOSMs), developed to promote the quality of content
disseminated on these platforms, are largely influenced by
the social interactions of users on the platform. These
systems face numerous challenges, including the prevalence
of bots and potential manipulation of voting activities. Such
issues can inadvertently undermine the very objectives for
which these platforms were designed. For instance, they may
facilitate the exploitation of reward systems for undeserved
gains, even in the presence of spammy content, while
also contributing to the creation of echo chambers and
information bubbles. Therefore, it is essential to be able to
accurately identify the quality of content that is rewarded
on BOSMs.
To contribute to this aim, in this article we provided a
study concerning the actual relationship between the quality
of the content disseminated in the context of BOSMs, and
the rewards provided to users by their reward systems.
We considered distinct sets of features characterizing quality
content in social media, in association with distinct classifiers
to identify content actually worthy of reward. To both
perform and asses such a study, we exploited a real
dataset, collected from Steemit, one of the most well-known
decentralized social media platforms based on blockchain
technology. Specifically, we hired human assessors to label
the dataset with respect to reward-deservingness classes,
to both train and evaluate distinct configurations of the
different feature sets in association with the considered
classifiers.
Based on the obtained findings, we have demonstrated
that it is indeed possible to effectively assess the quality
of content circulated on BOSMs by considering content
quality features in association with machine learning. This
capability has been utilized in this paper to distinguish
between content that truly deserves rewards and that which
does not. Through this analysis, we have shown that,
actually, current reward systems do not fulfill their intended
purpose of promoting the highest-quality content through
reward strategies. Consequently, these systems fall short
of functioning as de facto bottom-up content moderation
mechanisms.
A challenging research direction could involve proposing
and formalizing a reward system that incorporates content
analysis characteristics to effectively distribute rewards

Section: Implementation Paper: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in Blockchain Online Social Media implementation of moderation policies imposed by administrators. Another important study investigates whether and to what extent toxic content spreads in Pleroma, [34] and how to tackle this issue by proposing a system forcollaborative moderation. Federated DOSNs can apply some policies for content moderation thanks to the usage of servers. Among such platforms, Mastodon is probably the most studied one, thanks to the possibility of retrieving social content. In [35], in particular, the decentralised content moderation strategy employed by Mastodon is analyzed, where moderation is performed by specific users, and it is always applied locally, on the server instance. Specifically, so-called blocklisting servers prevent users of an instance from interacting with the content of servers spreading harmful or inappropriate content. BOSMs have the possibility of employing both ‘‘tra- ditional’’ decentralized content moderation strategies and reward strategies as a proxy to incentivize valuable content. Referring to the first approach, since BOSMs like Steemit are based on social blockchains where content is publicly available, they can simply operate on hiding certain content 13https://www.theguardian.com/media/2021/may/26/pro-palestine- censorship-facebook-instagramon their front end. In Hive, as previously introduced, born from a fork of the Steem blockchain, many members of the community have evolved certain mechanisms and projects for moderating content, even if content removal is not possible. Minds applies a jury system instead of a content moderation team to check appeals for reported content. The jury system aims to involve users and give them a voice in deciding on the appropriateness of the content. Every time a post is moderated, the original author of the post can appeal as to why the decision should be changed. The appeal is sent to twelve unique active users who are not subscribed to the reported channel. If 75% of the jury votes in favor of the appeal, then the initial decision is overturned. Otherwise, the initial decision is upheld. The jury can not ban or remove content, it is about building a democratic tagging system. In DeSo, every node that runs on top of the DeSo blockchain, including dApps, can expose whatever subset of the posts that they want, without showing illegal or harmful content. That content would still technically be on the blockchain, but not practically accessible. In this article, however, we focus on the second approach, i.e., the fact that BOSMs can incentivize

DeSo blockchain, including dApps, can expose whatever subset of the posts that they want, without showing illegal or harmful content. That content would still technically be on the blockchain, but not practically accessible. In this article, however, we focus on the second approach, i.e., the fact that BOSMs can incentivize quality content based on reward systems. Indeed, as previously introduced, rewarding systems have been proposed also to solve this issue [3]. However, their potential is not fully exploited because the current reward strategies do not take into account in any way quality characteristics of the published content and related semantic aspects when assigning rewards. Only in a recent work [36]the authors propose a study concerning crypto-reward fairness on user engagement. In this paper, the authors analyze the impact of fairness in the distribution of cryptocurrency rewards on user content creation in Steemit focusing on the Key entities: FAC: Steemit CARDINAL: 3, 34, 35, 13https://www.theguardian.com/media/2021/may/26/pro-palestine-, twelve GPE: Pleroma, DeSo PERSON: Mastodon, Hive PRODUCT: Steem PERCENT: 75% ORG: DeSo, dApps

However, also in this paper, the analysis of content quality is not taken into consideration when awarding a reward. III. EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF BOSM REWARD SYSTEM BASED ON CONTENT ANALYSIS A first, important part of this study is devoted to analyze the quality of content with respect to its being reward- worthy; this concerns the identification and analysis of quality features that can be taken into consideration for this purpose. Hence, the main feature categories that have been VOLUME 13, 2025 21931 D. Mancino et al.: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in BOSM used in the literature for content quality analysis in social media are introduced in this section. Subsequently, they are employed in association with different classifiers to identify quality UGC, and to assess the effectiveness of reward systems in BOSMs concerning this aspect. In particular, we consider, within each feature category, those features that can be effectively extracted from theSteemit platform, which is used in this article to both model and evaluate the considered solutions. A. FEATURE ENGINEERING The six considered feature categories include the so-called: (i)stylistic features, (ii) readability features, (iii) grammar features, (iv) opinion-based features, (v) metadata features, and (vi) textual representation features. 1) STYLISTIC FEATURES They are characteristics that are related to the identification ofpatterns, trends, and/or statistics in texts [37]. They have been considered in several solutions for UGC quality analysis in OSNEMs, such as [25], [38], [39], and [40]. They include text length, the presence of specific characters, the number of words, syllables, sentences, etc. For this work, we considered the following features. 1)length: the length in characters of the text of a post; 2)words: the number of words in the text of a post; 3)sentences: the number of sentences in the text of a post; 4)syllables_count: the number of syllables in the text of a post; 5)word_sentence: the number of words over the number of sentences; 6)syllables_word: the number of syllables over the number of words; 7)characters_word: the number of characters over the number of words; 8)unique_word: the number of unique words; 9)n_spaces: the number of spaces in the text; 10)n_consonants: the number of consonants in the text; 11)n_numbers: is equal to the count of the numbers in the text; 12)contains_?: is

the number of words; 7)characters_word: the number of characters over the number of words; 8)unique_word: the number of unique words; 9)n_spaces: the number of spaces in the text; 10)n_consonants: the number of consonants in the text; 11)n_numbers: is equal to the count of the numbers in the text; 12)contains_?: is equal to 1 if the text contains ?, instead, is 0; 13)contains_!: is equal to 1 if the text contains !, instead, is 0; 14)multiple_!: is equal to 1 if the text contains multiple !, instead, is 0; 15)multiple_?: is equal to 1 if the text contains multiple ?, instead, is 0; 16)multiple_...: is equal to 1 if the text contains a sequence of more than three ., instead, is 0; 17)spaces_length: the number of spaces over the length of the text in characters; 18)avg_l_words: the average length of words; 19)avg_l_sentences: the average length of sentences; 20)n_punctuation: number of punctuation characters;21)punctuation_length: the number of punctuation char- acters over the length of the text in characters; 22)correct_capitalization: if the text is correctly formatted. 2) READABILITY FEATURES They measure the complexity of the written language and the ease of understanding for the intended audience. They can be computed by using factors such as word length and complexity, sentence structure, and syllables, and by means of suitable readability indexes [25], [41]. In fact, distinct readability indexes have been proposed and employed in the literature for UGC quality assessment in OSNEMs, such as[25] and[38]. In this work, we employed two of the most popular ones, i.e., (i) the Gunning fog index [42], and (ii) the Flesch Reading Ease formula [41]. The two readabil- ity features that were obtained are as follows: 23)gunning_fog: the Gunning fog index estimates the years of formal education a person needs to understand an English text on the first reading. The equation to compute this index is defined as: grade_level =0.4(ASL +PHW) where ASL indicates the Average Sentence Length, i.e., the number of words divided by the number of sentences, and PHW indicates the Percentage of Hard Words. Hard words are all the words of three or more syllables that are not proper nouns, combinations of easy words or hyphenated words, or two-syllable verbs made into three with -es and -ed endings; 24)flesch: the Flesch Reading Ease formula assesses the difficulty of reading passages written in English. It is calculated using the average number of syllables per word and the

are not proper nouns, combinations of easy words or hyphenated words, or two-syllable verbs made into three with -es and -ed endings; 24)flesch: the Flesch Reading Ease formula assesses the difficulty of reading passages written in English. It is calculated using the average number of syllables per word and the average number of words per sentence in a text. The formula produces a value ranging from 0 to 100, where a high value means that the text is easy to read. It is formally defined as: RE=206.835 −(1.015 ·ASL)−(84.6·ASW) where RE stands for Readability Ease, ASL indicates theAverage Sentence Length, i.e., the number of words divided by the number of sentences; ASW indicates theAverage number of Syllables per Word, i.e., the number of syllables divided by the number of words. Scores between 90.0 and 100.0 indicate an easily understandable text by an average 4th grader. Scores between 60.0 and 70.0 easily understandable text by 7th and 8th graders. Scores between 0.0 and 30.0 an easily understandable text by college graduates. 3) GRAMMAR FEATURES They capture grammatical aspects of texts that can be interpreted as quality indicators. In fact, by counting the number of adverbs, pronouns, nouns, verbs, adjectives, and other Parts of Speech (PoS) in the text, we can gain insights 21932 VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Mancino et al.: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in BOSM into the overall structure and coherence of the writing. The use of such features has proven to be a powerful tool for identifying the quality of UGC in text mining applications [43], [44]. We consider the following grammar features. 25)n_adverbs: the number of adverbs in the text; 26)n_verbs: the number of verbs in the text; 27)n_adjectives: the number of adjectives in the text; 28)n_pronouns: the number of pronouns in the text; 29)n_nouns: the number of nouns in the text; 30)adjectives_adverbs: the number of adjectives plus the number of adverbs. 4) OPINION-BASED FEATURES They are able to characterize textual content based on the author’s stance and the sentiment transmitted. We considered, in particular, features related to: (i) text polarity by means of sentiment analysis, which gives us insight into how positive, neutral, or negative a text is; (ii) text emotion by means ofemotion recognition, for the identification of specific emotions from a text; and (iii) offensive language by means ofoffensive language identification, which gives information on whether a text contains offensive

of sentiment analysis, which gives us insight into how positive, neutral, or negative a text is; (ii) text emotion by means ofemotion recognition, for the identification of specific emotions from a text; and (iii) offensive language by means ofoffensive language identification, which gives information on whether a text contains offensive content or not. The usage of opinion-based features has been already tested in the literature for evaluating the quality of UGC in OSNEMs, such as in [38]and[43]. In this article, we used contextual models to build the considered features [45], which are further detailed later in the scenario of textual representation features. a: SENTIMENT ANALYSIS FEATURES For the sentiment analysis task, we used a semantic-based approach, i.e., a roBERTa-based model [46], trained on ∼124M tweets from January 2018 to December 2021 [47], and fine-tuned for sentiment analysis with the TweetEval benchmark [48], constituted of social media content. Addi- tionally, to implement this model for sentiment analysis tasks, we leveraged the Python Transformers library.14The goal of this task is to extract the sentiment polarity of a text, which can be classified into positive, neutral, and negative. For this reason, we built the following three features. 31)positive: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the sentiment of the text is positive; 32)neutral: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the sentiment of the text is neutral; 33)negative: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the sentiment of the text is negative. b: EMOTION RECOGNITION FEATURES Also for the emotion recognition task, we used the roBERTa-based model from the Transformers library, trained on∼58M tweets, and fine-tuned for emotion recognition with the TweetEval benchmark. This task consists of recognizing the emotion evoked by a tweet. This model of 14https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/indexemotion recognition assigns to posts the most common four emotions among anger, joy,sadness, and optimism. Hence, we generated the following four features. 34)anger: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text expresses anger; 35)joy: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or∼1, the text expresses joy; 36)sadness: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text expresses

is 1 or ∼1, the text expresses anger; 35)joy: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or∼1, the text expresses joy; 36)sadness: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text expresses sadness; 37)optimism: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text expresses optimism. c: OFFENSIVE LANGUAGE IDENTIFICATION FEATURES Also for this task, we used the roBERTa-based model, trained on ∼58M tweets, and fine-tuned with the TweetEval benchmark. This task consists of identifying whether some form of offensive language is present in a piece of text. In this work, we focus just on the presence or the absence of offensive language, therefore we extract only two features. 38)offensive: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text is offensive; 39)not_offensive: it is a number in a range between 0 and 1. If it is 1 or ∼1, the text is not offensive. 5) METADATA FEATURES They are related to data and metadata that can be present in, or related to, a text. They allow capturing additional information regarding the content or their authors. In the literature, these features have been used for the quality analysis of UGC in OSNEMs [40], [49]. For this category, we extracted features mostly related to the content, i.e., the number of images and links included in the different posts, as well as, the category to which the posts belong, as follows. 40)n_img: the number of images in a post; 41)n_link: the number of links in a post; 42)category: often, posts in BOSM are categorized according to the topic to which the contents belong. In this case, instead of a single feature, we generated 33 dummy features representing the most frequently used categories in BOSMs (e.g., photography, life, travel, music, etc.). Such features will be illustrated in detail in Section IV-B2. 6) TEXTUAL REPRESENTATION FEATURES They are related to the numeric formal representation of the semi-structured/unstructured text constituting the social media content, to make it mathematically computable. This feature category has also been used in the literature to analyze UGC [50], [51]. We describe how we built two categories of textual representation features, using the currently most advanced textual representation models, i.e., contextualized word embedding models such

constituting the social media content, to make it mathematically computable. This feature category has also been used in the literature to analyze UGC [50], [51]. We describe how we built two categories of textual representation features, using the currently most advanced textual representation models, i.e., contextualized word embedding models such as BERT [52], ERNIE [53], and MISTRAL [54], which allow semantic and contextual aspects of the text to be taken into account [45]. Hence, such categories are denoted as: (i) BERT-based representation VOLUME 13, 2025 21933 D. Mancino et al.: Striking the Balance: Evaluating Content Quality and Reward Dynamics in BOSM features, (ii) ERNIE-based representation features, and (iii)MISTRAL-based representation features. a: BERT-BASED REPRESENTATION FEATURES Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT), already used for the extraction of opinion-based fea- tures (i.e., via the roBERTa-based model), is a Transformer- based language model developed by Google, with a variable number of encoder layers and self-attention heads. In our framework, given an input text, BERT is employed to produce a dense vector representation of the input that considers semantic similarity among words. Specifically, the model is a pre-trained DistilroBERTa-based model [55], which is fine-tuned over a 1 billion sentence pairs dataset. The model maps each text to a 768-dimensional dense feature vector that can be used for tasks like classification or semantic search. b: ERNIE-BASED REPRESENTATION FEATURES ERNIE 2.0 is a continual pre-training framework pro- posed by Baidu in 2019, which builds and learns incre- mentally pre-training tasks through constant multi-task learning that can be used to learn lexical, syntactic, and semantic information from several different tasks [56]. Like the BERT-based representation model employed for extracting text representation features in the above- mentioned case, the ERNIE-base model maps texts to a 768-dimensional dense vector space. So, also in this case, we produce a 768-dimensional dense feature vector for the text of each post. c: MISTRAL-BASED REPRESENTATION FEATURES The model used is e5-mistral-7b-instruct [57]initialized from Mistral-7B-v0.1 a Large Language Model with 7 billion parameters [54]. Specifically, this model was fine-tuned on synthetic data generated by LLMs and a mixture of multilingual datasets to generate text embedding [58]. This MISTRAL-based model maps texts to a 4,096-dimensional dense vector space. So, for the text of each post, we produce a 4,096-dimensional dense feature vector. B. QUALITY-BASED CLASSIFICATION OF CONTENT This second phase constitutes, along with the feature engi- neering phase, the core of the proposed analysis. In

embedding [58]. This MISTRAL-based model maps texts to a 4,096-dimensional dense vector space. So, for the text of each post, we produce a 4,096-dimensional dense feature vector. B. QUALITY-BASED CLASSIFICATION OF CONTENT This second phase constitutes, along with the feature engi- neering phase, the core of the proposed analysis. In fact, it is at this stage that distinct classifiers are employed, in association with the aforementioned quality features, to evaluate whether the content produced by users on BOSM platforms is truly worthy of reward. The strategy considered is therefore about performing classification tasks that can assign posts generated within BOSMs to distinct categories of reward-deservingness. In general, with any supervised model, we try to learn a function that maps an input to an output based on example input-output pairs. In the solution proposed in this work, we exploit a subset of the available labeled data (the labeling process is described in Section IV-A1) as training set to train FIGURE 1. The supervised learning strategy to identify distinct classes of reward-deservingness to be associated with BOSM posts. five different classifiers, based on well-known supervised machine learning techniques, i.e., Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, Logistic Regression, Multilayer Perceptron, andXGBoost. The test set consists of the remaining part of the available labeled dataset. The pipeline of such a strategy is illustrated in Figure 1. IV. EXPERIMENTAL EVALUATION This section is devoted to illustrate distinct dimensions related to the performed experimental evaluation. DAVIDE MANCINO received the M.Sc. degree in data science from the Department of Computer Science, Systems, and Communication, Università degli Studi di Milano-Bicocca, Italy, in 2022. He is currently pursuing the joint Ph.D. degree (Italian National Ph.D. in Blockchain) with the University of Camerino, Italy, and the Department of Computer Science, Systems, and Communi- cation, Università degli Studi di Milano-Bicocca, Italy. His research interests include blockchain and blockchain online social media, social network analysis, data science, and natural language processing. BARBARA GUIDI received the B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees in computer science from the University of Pisa, Italy, in 2007, 2011, and 2015, respectively. She is currently an Assistant Profes- sor with the Department of Computer Science, Università degli Studi di Pisa. Her current research interests include decentralized online social net- works, blockchain technology, and social network analysis. She has been involved in the TPC of several international conferences and workshops. She was the Co-Chair of the conference EAI GoodTechs 2017 and several workshops. She has been a Reviewer of relevant journals, such as IEEE ACCESS. ANDREA MICHIENZI received the Ph.D. degree in computer science from the Department of Computer Science, University of Pisa, Italy, in July 2021. He is currently an Assistant Professor with the Department of Computer Science, Università degli Studi di Pisa. His main research interests include the dynamics of socio-economic networks, enabling technologies for the metaverse, and the application of blockchain to social networking platforms. He is

University of Pisa, Italy, in July 2021. He is currently an Assistant Professor with the Department of Computer Science, Università degli Studi di Pisa. His main research interests include the dynamics of socio-economic networks, enabling technologies for the metaverse, and the application of blockchain to social networking platforms. He is one of the organizers of the Open Challenges in Online Social Networks Workshop. MARCO VIVIANI received the M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees in computer science from the Univer- sity of Milan-La Statale, Italy. Later, he was a Postdoctoral Fellow at both the University of Insubria, Italy, and foreign institutions (University of Burgundy, INSA Lyon, France). He is currently an Associate Professor with the Department of Informatics Systems, and Communication, Uni- versità degli Studi di Milano-Bicocca, Italy. He is involved in organizing several research initiatives at the international level. His main research interests include social computing, information retrieval, natural language processing, security, privacy, and trust. On these topics, he has published more than 90 research works in international journals, international conferences, and monographs and book chapters. He was the General Co-Chair of MDAI 2019 and organized several workshops and special tracks at international conferences. He is an Associate Editor of Social Network Analysis and Mining and Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence-Natural Language Processing and an Area Editor (Web Intelligence and E-Services) of the International Journal of Computational Intelligence Systems. Paper ID: P02
Title: Deep Multi-Source Visual Fusion With Transformer Model for Video Content Filtering
Authors: Senthil Murugan Nagarajan, Ganesh Gopal Devarajan, Asha Jerlin M, Daniel Arockiam, Ali Kashif Bashir, and Maryam M. Al Dabel
Year: 2025
Published in: IEEE JOURNAL OF SELECTED TOPICS IN SIGNAL PROCESSING
Domain: Artificial Intelligence, transformer model, deep learning
Keywords: Multi-modal fusion, transformer model, deep learning, content filtering, speech enhancement.

Section: Conclusion
Paper: Deep Multi-Source Visual Fusion With Transformer Model for Video Content Filtering

conclusion with some future scope of this
research is discussed in Section VI.
II. R ELATED WORKS
Several studies have explored the recommendation of me-
dia advertisements and content ﬁltering based on social rel-
evance [13],[14]. The design and production of multimedia
content play a crucial role in feature extraction analysis, as
high-level patterns in temporal and multimedia features often
mimic human cognition. This requires a deep understanding
of both applications and content creation [15],[16]. Kuleshov
et al. [17] analyzed the impact of ﬁltering during pre-processing,
focusing primarily on band-limited input data preparation for
audio super-resolution before down-sampling.
Cambria et al. [18] introduced Sentic-Blending, which com-
bines modalities for emotion-based content. Their model com-
bined natural language text and facial expressions to track

However, none of the existing benchmarks speciﬁcally addresses the proposed video classiﬁcation problem. Data sets including the Elsagate dataset and the NPDI cartoon dataset. Rather, Elsagate dataset is a publicly available dataset that contains cartoon video and best candidate dataset to address our issue in which each video is clas- siﬁed as safe or unsafe, but in this dataset even clear frame is also labeled as unsafe that misleads to mis-classiﬁcation. Moreover, it lacks the intricate behaviors associated with sensitive content. However, the NPDI dataset consists of only 900 images, which is too small and unsuitable for our deep learning-based video classiﬁcation task. Anime videos serve as a suitable dataset for our experiments, being animated videos containing intermittent indecent content. Each anime series consists of a varying number of episodes, typically lasting 20 to 25 minutes. As our focus is on ﬁne-grained detection, the dataset needs to include short- duration video clips. Consequently, we divided each episode into one-second duration clips, resulting in 109,835 video clips, encompassing all episodes in that series. To establish ground truth, a video annotation portal was developed. The annotators, which consisted of ten undergraduate and graduate students aged 20 to 25, both male and female, received detailed instructions on the annotation task. Upon logging in, they were presented with a list of videos to watch. During viewing, they were tasked with categorizing each video clip as safe, depicting violence, or containing sexual content. Clips featuring other acts (e.g., extreme bloodshed or violence, smoking, drug use, frightening or horror scenes, etc.) were excluded from this dataset. The manual annotation process resulted in a total of 111,561 video clips, including 57,908 clips classiﬁed as safe, 27,003 clips in the sexual-nudity class, and 26,650 clips in the fantasy violence class. Section: Methodology
Paper: Deep Multi-Source Visual Fusion With Transformer Model for Video Content Filtering

methodology against
leading video descriptors and classiﬁers. This benchmark
sheds light on its competitive advantages and potential
areas for further optimization, informs future research di-
rections, and contributes to the advancement of the ﬁeld.
In the constant ﬁght to protect children online, understand-
ing the intricacies of child safety detection models is crucial.
This research dives into the relationship between temporal
granularity and the performance of our proposed MFusTSVD
model, speciﬁcally its effectiveness in pinpointing subtle child
safety concerns. Using meticulously segmented video clips of
varying lengths (12, 7, 4, and 2 seconds), we trained separate
MFusTSVD instances to capture the intricacies of temporal
context at different scales. To maximize individual learning, we
employ a transfer learning approach, initializing the encoder
weights of a ﬁnal, ﬁne-tuned classiﬁer with those of each indi-
vidual model.
As depicted in Fig. 2, we observed a signiﬁcant upward trend
in precision, recall, and AUC values as clip size decreased. This
suggests that the MFusTSVD model thrives on shorter durations,
effectively harnessing localized temporal context to pinpoint
child safety concerns with remarkable precision. Fig. 3further
strengthens this observation, showcasing individual ROC curves
for each clip size. We witnessed a steady upward ascension
with decreasing clip lengths, particularly pronounced for the
“sexual” class of harmful content. This potentially indicates an
advantage of focusing on immediate actions within smaller win-
dows for speciﬁc types of child exploitation, potentially avoidingextraneous information or redundancy that might be introduced
by broader contexts. Balancing granularity with feasibility, we
judiciously set the minimum clip size at 2 seconds. This provides
sufﬁcient temporal context for accurate differentiation while
remaining practical for annotation and resource management.
B. Bench-Marking Against Established Techniques
The chosen models include prominent architectures such
as CNNs (VGG-19, ResNet, Combined), RNNs (Memory Fu-
sion Network (MFN), Early Fusion LSTM (EF-LSTM), Late
Fusion LSTM (LF-LSTM)), Transformer-based architectures
(multi-modal Transformer (MulT), Interpretable multi-modal
Routing (IMR)), Tensor fusion-based

Security Analysis of MBAC MBAC effectively mitigates various security threats by lever- aging the security properties of FBME. First, MBAC ensures data conﬁdentiality through FBME’s privacy property, allowing only authorized receivers with valid secret keys to decrypt the ci-phertext, thus preventing unauthorized access

the proof in the appendix. /squaresolid B. Security Analysis of MBAC MBAC effectively mitigates various security threats by lever- aging the security properties of FBME. First, MBAC ensures data conﬁdentiality through FBME’s privacy property, allowing only authorized receivers with valid secret keys to decrypt the ci-phertext, thus preventing unauthorized access from both external and internal adversaries. Moreover, MBAC preserves receiver anonymity within FBME’s privacy, ensuring that neither theservice provider nor unauthorized users can deduce the set of privileged receivers from the ciphertext or reporting process, which is essential for privacy in multi-receiver settings. Thisanonymity also prevents the service provider from linking spe- ciﬁc senders and receivers during individual communications, even with knowledge of the public keys of all registered users.In addition, MBAC resists impersonation attacks by leveraging FBME’s authenticity, ensuring only legitimate data senders can produce valid ciphertexts, and preventing malicious parties frominjecting forged data. For content moderation, MBAC provides a veriﬁable re- porting mechanism via FBME’s accountability. Sender binding Authorized licensed use limited to: Sri Venkateswara College of Engineering. WANG et al.: CONTENT-MODERATED BILATERAL ACCESS CONTROL FOR PRIV ACY-PRESERVING CLOUD DATA SHARING SERVICES 1407 TABLE II COMPARISON OF COMPUTATIONAL COST TABLE III COMPARISON OF STORAGE COST prevents senders from altering ciphertexts to evade moderation, while receiver binding ensures receivers cannot submit fraudu- lent reports to frame innocent senders. Any report of malicious content is cryptographically tied to its sender, making the report- ing process tamper-proof and resistant to abuse. Lastly, MBAC offers post-compromise protection through FBME’s deniability. In case of a breach, senders can deny public attribution of speciﬁc data unless explicitly veriﬁed by the service provider, thus protecting their reputation and mitigating potential repercus- sions. By incorporating these robust security properties, MBAC effectively resists various attack vectors. VII. Paper ID: P04
Title: Text Moderation in Online Communities: Integrating User Attributes and Interaction Graph Embedding
Authors: Dong Jing, Xu Gao, and Kee-Hung Lai
Year: 2025
Published in: BIG DATA MINING AND ANALYTICS
Domain: Natural Language Processing, Deep Learning, and Graph-Based Social Network Analysis
Keywords: text moderation; pre-trained model; online community; user attributes; interaction graph embedding

Section: Conclusion
Paper: Text Moderation in Online Communities: Integrating User Attributes and Interaction Graph Embedding

Conclusion
In this paper, we address the practical requirements of
text moderation in online communities by proposing a
novel  text  moderation  model.  Building  upon  the
existing  pre-trained  text  classification  model,  we
integrate  the  community  users’ attributes  and  the
community  user  interaction  features  extracted  by
Node2Vec  to  enhance  the  effectiveness  of  text
moderation.  By  considering  community  user  features,
our model gains valuable insights to accurately identify
toxic  content,  presenting  a  novel  approach  to  online
community  text  moderation.  Furthermore,  we  conduct
an  in-depth  exploration  of  the  impact  of  community
user  attributes  and  community  user  interaction  graph
embedding on the content moderation effectiveness of
the  model.  This  research  opens  new  possibilities  for
more  effective  and  efficient  text  moderation  in  online
communities.

Section: Discussion
Paper: Structural Synchronization in Classification of Controversial Content

Discussion
Individuals  vary  substantially  in  how  they  categorize
novel and controversial content, as indicated both by
experimental  psychological  research[3–6] and  by
qualitative  and  quantitative  analyses  of  content
moderators  in  social  media  organizations[8–14].  Yet,
across  a  variety  of  contexts  and  topics,  independent
groups of social media users demonstrate remarkable
consistency  in  how  they  categorize  controversial
content[19–26],  raising  the  question  of  how  such
consistency can arise at the collective level amid stark
individual  differences  in  classification.  Building  on
recent  work  in  collective  intelligence[7, 33, 37–40, 64],  we
show  that  when  people  engage  in  peer-coordinated
content  moderation  within  structurally  symmetrical
social networks, their decisions for how to categorize
and  remove  controversial  content  can  become
synchronized  across  independent  communities.
Importantly,  our  findings  are  a  direct  replication  of
prior  models  and  experiments  demonstrating “scale-
induced  convergence” in  the  categorization  of
arbitrary stimuli[7]. This work shows that the dynamics
of scale enables replicable trajectories of classification
to  emerge  despite  substantial  individual  variation  in
perception; specifically, these studies show how scale
can induce synchronization in classification even when
no particular classification is preferred by the majority
of the population. This shows how scale plays a driving
role  in  inducing  synchronization  in  classification,
distinct from majority reinforcement.
A  core  strength  of  our  study  is  its  use  of  a
randomized  controlled  design  at  the  network-level,
which  enables  precise  identification  of  the  effects  of
scale  on  convergence  dynamics  in  classification.
However, this design requires compromising elements
of  realism  that  mark  notable  limitations  of  our  study
and key areas for future research. First, our study only
examines  a  single  network  topology  in  large  teams,
namely  a  fully-connected  (or  homogeneously  mixing)
population. This topology is the ideal starting place for

Section: Introduction Paper: Structural Synchronization in Classification of Controversial Content Introduction Individuals vary substantially in how they categorizenovel phenomena[1–3] and particularly in how they interpret emotionally charged[4, 5] and morally controversial content[6] such as offensive and hateful images over social media. This individual variation is captured not only by experimental research[2, 3, 7], but also by qualitative and quantitative analyses of people who work as content moderators specializing in the practice of categorizing and removing controversial content that violates the community standards of social media[8–14]. Congress[15] and the United Nations[16], research shows that even trained content moderators consistently disagree in their decisions for how to classify hate speech and offensive images, and whether to remove this content[8, 10–14, 17, 18]. Yet, puzzlingly, recent large-scale analyses of classification patterns over social media find that distinct populations of internet users (e.g., Democrats and Republicans) can arrive at similar categorizations of inflammatory content, such as partisan news and hate speech[19–26], despite considerable differences in their political orientations and moral reasoning[27–29]. This raises an important puzzle: How can separate and diverse populations of social media users arrive at similar ways of categorizing controversial content, despite considerable differences in their interpretation of and approach to such content? Here, we provide novel insights into this puzzle by investigating how interacting in social networks can filter individual variation and lead separate networks (or “teams”) to arrive at highly similar classifications of controversial content, a phenomenon we refer to as structural synchronization. Our theory is motivated by recent studies of collective intelligence which find that exchanging information in structured social networks reduces variation in individual judgments within populations, while also leading to convergent solutions across populations[30–37]. In particular, a recent study by Guilbeault et al.[7] in 2021 finds that distinct populations with similar network

is motivated by recent studies of collective intelligence which find that exchanging information in structured social networks reduces variation in individual judgments within populations, while also leading to convergent solutions across populations[30–37]. In particular, a recent study by Guilbeault et al.[7] in 2021 finds that distinct populations with similar network structures independently evolve highly similar classification schemes for categorizing novel visual stimuli—viz., the same stimuli for which isolated individuals produced highly varied and inconsistent classification schemes. This prior work emphasizes the role of structure— and specifically, the structural feature of scale—as the driving mechanism behind synchronization, since these prior studies find that interactions in small groups are insufficient for inducing replicable patterns of classification. IV . E XPERIMENTS
A. Experimental Setup
In this section, we conduct experiments to verify the effec-
tiveness of our proposed method.
All data used in our experiments are collected in a real industry
scenario. The training data are composed of 33896 illegal ASMR
erotica audios, and large number of normal audios since the
normal audios are easier to gather. But in the experiment, we
limit the number of normal audios to 200654, and make use of
all these normal audios by default.
To ensure the test set can reﬂect the model performance in a
real application, we directly sampled test data from an online
business platform and then tag labels manually to simulate the
distribution of real data. The test set we gathered includes 4122
illegal audios and 652801 normal audios, with a ratio of roughly
proportionality to 1:158. The sampling rate of all these audios
is 16 kHz and the maximum duration is 15 s. For audios shorter
than 15 s, we padded zeros to make the duration consistent.
We will use precision, recall and F1 as the evaluation met-
ric. By regarding ASMR erotica audios as the positive class,
precision and recall are deﬁned in [26].

Arya et al.: MSCMGTB: A Novel Approach for Multimodal Social Media Content Moderation Adding another layer of sophistication, the model embraces Genetic Algorithms (GAs) for hyper-parameter optimization, fine-tuning the model’s parameters to peak performance. This optimization is not a mere adjustment of variables but a strategic enhancement, streamlining the model to adapt and respond with increased precision. The MSCMGTB model’s true prowess, however, is epitomized in its use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), enabling it to discern the intricate intra and inter-modal relationships inherent in the scenarios of social media contents. This capability is not just a function of data processing but a testament to the model’s advanced analytical acumen, capable of navigating and interpreting the complex web of interconnected content elements with remarkable efficiency levels. To evaluate the efficiency and effectiveness of the proposed MSCMGTB model for multimodal social media content moderation, an extensive experimental setup was designed. This setup encompasses the utilization of three distinct datasets: the Hate Speech Dataset Catalogue, DMO Social Media Engagement Dataset, and ZENPULSAR - Social Media Pulse Data Set: CRYPTO. Each dataset contributes uniquely to the assessment of the model’s capabilities. A. DATASET DESCRIPTION •Hate Speech Dataset Catalogue: This dataset is a com- prehensive collection of textual data specifically tailored to identify and analyze hate speech across various social media platforms. It includes approximately 200,000 anno- tated posts and comments, categorized into different classes of hate speech. The dataset is diverse, encompassing multiple languages and regions, thereby offering a challenging envi- ronment for the MSCMGTB model to demonstrate its text analysis and contextual understanding capabilities. •DMO Social Media Engagement Dataset: Encompass- ing a wide range of image and video content, this dataset is pivotal for assessing the visual content analysis strength of MSCMGTB. It contains over 300,000 multimedia posts from various social media platforms, tagged with engagement metrics like likes, shares, and comments. This dataset aids in understanding how visual content correlates with user engagement and sentiment. •ZENPULSAR - Social Media Pulse Data Set: CRYPTO: This

the visual content analysis strength of MSCMGTB. It contains over 300,000 multimedia posts from various social media platforms, tagged with engagement metrics like likes, shares, and comments. This dataset aids in understanding how visual content correlates with user engagement and sentiment. •ZENPULSAR - Social Media Pulse Data Set: CRYPTO: This dataset is unique as it provides sentiment data extracted from over 0.5 billion data points across seven major social media platforms. It focuses on the cryptocurrency domain, offering insights into public sentiment on social media about various cryptocurrencies. The dataset’s volume and domain-specific nature make it ideal for testing the MSCMGTB model’s ability to process and analyze large- scale, topic-specific social media content. B. MODEL PARAMETERS SET-UP •Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) Parameters: For image and video content analysis, the CNNs were configured with a learning rate of 0.001, a batch size of 32, and dropout rate of 0.5 to prevent overfitting. The CNNs employed a totalof 5 convolutional layers, each followed by a max-pooling layer. •Transformers Parameters: For textual content analysis, the Transformers were set up with a model size of 768, 12 attention heads, and 3 encoder-decoder layers. A token limit of 512 was set for processing textual inputs. •Bi-directional Attention Mechanism (BAM) Parameters: BAM was integrated to dynamically prioritize information from textual and visual inputs. The attention mechanism had a dimensionality of 1024 and used a softmax activation function to balance the focus between modalities. •Genetic Algorithms (GAs) for Hyperparameter Opti- mization: The population size was set to 50, with a mutation rate of 0.1 and a crossover probability of 0.9. The GAs ran for 100 generations to optimize the model’s performance parameters. •Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) Parameters: GNNs were employed to discern complex intra and inter-modal relation- ships. The GNN layer count was set to 3, with a hidden layer size of 256. C. SIMULATION ENVIRONMENT & PERFORMANCE METRICS The experiments were conducted on a high-performance computing cluster with NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPUs, 128 GB RAM, and Intel Xeon Gold 6130 CPUs. This setup ensured the timely processing of large datasets and the seamless exe- cution of deep learning models. This experimental setup, with its comprehensive datasets and meticulously chosen parameters, was crucial in rigor- ously evaluating the MSCMGTB model. The diverse nature of the datasets, along with the robust computational resources and evaluation metrics, provided a thorough and effective platform for assessing the model’s capabilities in

Section: Conclusion
Paper: A Hypersensitive Intelligent Filter for Detecting Explicit Content in Learning Environments

Conclusion
This research study presents the development of a content filtering model
designed specifically for streaming education platforms. The model utilizes
a combination of computational intelligence techniques to automatically
and instantly filter out violent and adult content. A unique aspect of this proposed system proposed system lies in its incorporation of deep learning, fuzzy logic and
advanced language models, marking the first instance of such integration in
the existing literature. This distinctive approach facilitates a comprehensive
analysis of digital content by considering both visual and fuzzy-linguistic
aspects.
To assess the effectiveness of our model, extensive experiments were
conducted employing an artificial dataset. The

Section: Implementation
Paper: A Hypersensitive Intelligent Filter for Detecting Explicit Content in Learning Environments

Implementation of our proposed model can
contribute to the creation of safer learning environments, particularly for
students, by successfully filtering out violent and adult content. This approach
reduces instances of false positives and false negatives, thereby augmenting
user satisfaction and bolstering trust in the content filtering capabilities of the
platform.
The integration of CNNs and IFS enhances accuracy in explicit content
detection by leveraging contextual understanding and effectively handling
ambiguity and borderline cases. Additionally, GPT-3’s generative warnings
A Hypersensitive Intelligent Filter for Detecting Explicit Content 107
play a crucial role in educating users and promoting safety. Moreover,
the system’s flexibility allows it to adapt to various content types and
keep up with emerging trends. On the other hand, the proposed method
is complex and requires specialized expertise. It relies on computationally
intensive AI techniques, which may limit its use in resource-constrained
environments. Gathering a substantial dataset and ensuring its privacy can
be time-consuming and ethically challenging. The system’s interpretability is
reduced, making it difficult to understand its classification decisions. Using
pre-trained models introduces external dependencies and potential licensing
costs. The system’s hypersensitivity to ambiguity can result in false positives
or false negatives, making it challenging to achieve the right balance.
Nevertheless, further research and development are necessary to address
the intricacies and challenges associated with content filtering, considering
the emergence of new types of inappropriate content. Prominent avenues
for future research encompass cross-linguistic analysis to evaluate content
filtering models across diverse languages and cultures, multimodal con-
tent analysis that integrates multiple modalities for comprehensive filtering,
examination of biases and fairness in content filtering models, contex-
tual analysis to enhance filtering based on surrounding content and user
interactions, the development of specialized models for deepfake detection,
real-time anomaly detection for emerging inappropriate content, analysis of
evolving content patterns and the creation of adaptive models, amalgamation
of AI-driven filtering with human moderation, privacy-preserving filtering

Introduction
In the present era of digital advancements, the accessibility of online content
has revolutionized the delivery and consumption of education [1]. How-
ever, alongside the numerous advantages offered by this digital landscape,
there are significant challenges in upholding secure learning environments.
An important concern involves the growing availability of explicit and inap-
propriate content, which poses risks to the well-being, mental health, and
overall educational experiences of students. Educational institutions bear the
crucial responsibility of safeguarding their students from exposure to harmful
content while fostering an environment conducive to learning and personal
development [2, 3].
To tackle this challenge, conventional

4R ISK-AWARE FRAMEWORK
This section ﬁrst presents a preliminary study that motivates our risk-
aware framework for multimodal video moderation. A detailed descrip-
tion of the framework and its two back-end components, namely, the
data-processing procedure and data-ﬁltering method, is followed.
4.1 Preliminary Study
The existing video moderation framework (Fig. 1) integrates a generic
reviewing tool [3] consisting of a video player with small multiples
of video frames (Fig. 7). The basic idea behind this framework is
that reviewing less information means better time performance. Thus,
moderators are required to review original video content with minimum
additional information, such as machine-generated scores. However,
we present a different assumption that enabling moderators to explore
multimodal video content and extracted risk information concurrently
may also improve their performance. We conducted a preliminary study
in collaboration with a moderation group in an e-commerce company
to validate this assumption.

Section: Discussion Paper: VideoModerator: A Risk-aware Framework for Multimodal Video Moderation in E-Commerce discussion about e-commerce moderation policies (Sec. 3). Five out of the 20 videos were normal videos, whereas the others were deviant videos. Video moderation refers to the process in which moderators ﬁrst review a video and then assign a label to the video. We deﬁned one normal label and four deviant labels that corresponded to the four mod- eration policies discussed in Sec. 3. Our task required the participants to moderate videos by assigning them a normal orrisklabel. We used two videos to demonstrate the two reviewing tools and enable the par- ticipants to practice. We used the rest of the videos for formal testing and obtained 18 tasks (T1-T18) in total. Procedure. The study consisted of three stages, namely, training ( 20 min), testing ( 30min), and interviewing ( 10min). At the ﬁrst stage, we demonstrated the basic concepts of video moderation and introduced the four policies that would be used to review videos. Consideringthat the participants had various backgrounds, we trained them withconcrete examples to remove the ambiguity and ensure that they had the same level of understandings about the policies. The testing stage was further divided into two similar phases ( 15min). At each phase, the participants were ﬁrst required to complete a practice task and then ﬁnish the formal tasks. During the practice task, the participants could freely explore reviewing tools and ask for our assistance at any time, which were not allowed for the formal tasks. We counterbalanced the order of two reviewing tools and tasks for a fair comparison. After the participants completed the formal tasks with one tool, they were asked G G H I Fig. 7. Section: Implementation Paper: VideoModerator: A Risk-aware Framework for Multimodal Video Moderation in E-Commerce Implementation We employ a client-server architecture to implement our mixed-initiative framework. A back-end server is developed to integrate various machine learning models, and a visualization interface is developed to facilitate multimodal video moderation. We implement the interface by using React.js, and develop visualizations by usingD3.js. We implement the back-end server in Python and develop themachine learning models with a state-of-the-art framework, namely,PyTorch. We deploy the back end on cloud computing devices and connect the client and server by using the web protocol HTTP. We conducted online experiments to understand the performance of the machine learning models incorporated in VideoModerator. The main goal of the experiments is to validate the effectiveness of the gen- eral framework instead of evaluating speciﬁc machine learning models. By collaborating with the e-commerce company, we deployed our back-end on high-performance computing clusters powered by elastic algorithm services1. We trained our models with the livestreaming videos reviewed by moderators to detect sensitive content, including abusing (audio) and explicit visual content (video). There were 21risk tags associated to explicit visual content and 11risk words related to abusing sentences. We mainly focused on these speciﬁc risks because covering full risks required a large amount of computational resources which were not accessible in the experiments. The trained models were tested using the real-world videos on our collaborators’ moderation platform. In total, our models processedaround 100thousands live streaming videos, including 0.4billions video frames and 36millions audio clips. The average accuracy of the models are 33.2% and35% for detecting audio and video risks, respectively. The recalling rate of the binary classiﬁer is 99.5% for ﬁltering possible deviant videos with abusing or explicit content. The model accuracy is far from the expectations due to the diversity and complexity of real-world video data. However, they are still acceptable according to our collaborators because platforms intend to have higher recalling rates, even by sacriﬁcing model accuracy in real practices. This strategy is understandable because the main goal of e-commerce platforms is to protect not prevent user-generated content. Thus, mod- erators would like to have things under control and review “possibly” deviant videos to avoid wrongly-detect videos as much as possible. 8U SEREVALUATION This section presents an in-lab user study to evaluate the user interface of VideoModerator by comparing it with a baseline method. to have things under control and review “possibly” deviant videos to avoid wrongly-detect videos as much as possible. 8U SEREVALUATION This section presents an in-lab user study to evaluate the user interface of VideoModerator by comparing it with a baseline method. Baseline. VideoModerator is established on the basis of idea of showing moderators’ additional but essential information, which can possibly increase their performance in reviewing videos. To validate this hypothesis, we conducted a task-based user study that compared VideoModerator with a baseline tool that merely presented original video content. Although many reviewing tools [2, 3] satisﬁed our requirements, we selected Content- Moderator [3] as the baseline method due to its detailed documents and intuitive interface (Fig. 7). We re-implemented ContentModerator, which comprised video and frame components, to make it suitable for our studies. Speciﬁcally, we changed the video component to make it similar to our video view and presented the same frames in Fig. 7a. Participants. Our experiment used a within-subject design. We recruited 12volunteers ( 6males and 6females) to complete two groups of video moderation tasks by using VideoModerator and the baseline tool. Their average age was 24.3 years old (range= 21-28years). All participants could read basic data visualizations, such as bar charts, and were knowledgeable about machine learning models. They were from various backgrounds, including computer science, social science, and industry design. Two of them had been involved in jobs related to video moderation, while the others knew it from news or social media. Each participant was paid 10$ for the 1-h study. Data and Tasks. To simulate the real environment of video modera- tion, we ﬁrst collected a bunch of videos that were detected as possibly deviant by machine learning models. We divided the videos into three groups in accordance with the video length, namely, the short group (<5min), the long group ( >30min), and the medium group (between two bounds). The video length indicated the difﬁculty of reviewing videos, in which longer videos usually required more reviewing efforts. To construct a

Section: Methodology Paper: VideoModerator: A Risk-aware Framework for Multimodal Video Moderation in E-Commerce methodology that employs two separate deep neural networks to learn subjective- andconceptual-level spatiotemporal information. Moreover, the authorsaggregated deep representations [74] under each speciﬁc concept bytraining a binary classiﬁer to moderate videos. Considering the mul-timodal features of videos, Wu et al. [68] proposed a neural networkthat contains holistic, localized, and score branches to determine de- viant videos by considering video and audio signals at different levels. Meanwhile, Giannakopoulos et al. [20] employed a “fusion” strategy combining audio and visual information to moderate video segments. Despite the advancements in deep learning-based video moderation techniques [6, 28, 56], using solely machine intelligence is insufﬁcient owing to the limitations of model generalization [14] and the changes in moderation guidelines [21]. For many online moderation appli-cations [2, 3], ﬁrst, machine learning models are employed to recallunderlying deviant videos; then, human moderators are recruited to review the videos closely. This two-step strategy intends to achieve an improved trade-off between preventing irregular content and protect-ing wrongfully identiﬁed videos. However, this strategy ignores the risk-aware information extracted by machine learning models, which is important for discovering deviant content. To promote an improved moderation paradigm, we propose a risk-aware framework that lever- ages human moderators with the deep analysis of machine-generated insights. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the ﬁrst to employ visual analytic approaches to moderate multimodal video content. 2.2 Video Visualization Video visualization has been investigated in the ﬁelds of multimedia analysis [13, 34, 75] and information visualization [7, 16, 42, 57, 64, 65, 69,71,72] for decades. We consider the relevant studies on visual video summarization and visual video analytics that are associated with video reviewing. 2.2.1 Visual Video Summarization Visual video summarization refers to the process of transforming a raw video into a compact visual form without losing substantial information. The goal of this process is to facilitate rapid video navigation by trans- forming video content and associated features into volume- [16, 38, 49], feature- [11, 18, 61], and context-based visualizations [8, 32, 46, 55].,ORZKXOTM 8K\OK]OTM2O\KYZXKGSOTM <OJKUY3GINOTK2KGXTOTM 3UJKRY .[SGT3UJKXGZUXY<OK]KXY*K\OGTZ<OJKUY Fig. 1. A typical mixed-initiative framework for video moderation. Videos are ﬁrstly ﬁltered by machine learning models and the suspicious videos are further reviewed by human moderators. Normal videos would be presented to public directly while deviant videos would be handled by different community policies [1, 4]. V olume-based video visualizations,

1. A typical mixed-initiative framework for video moderation. Videos are ﬁrstly ﬁltered by machine learning models and the suspicious videos are further reviewed by human moderators. Normal videos would be presented to public directly while deviant videos would be handled by different community policies [1, 4]. V olume-based video visualizations, which are also known as video graphics [7], employ volume rendering to transform video frames into a single graphic. Daniel et al. [16] formally proposed the idea of video visualization and employed different volume visualization techniques to visualize videos as volume cubes, which inspired several studies [38, 49]. For example, Kang et al. [38] proposed a space-time video montage that represents a video by using volumetric layers and combines highly informative portions into an integrated visualization. Nguyen et al. [49] developed VideoSummagator, which transforms a video into a space-time cube by combining the most informative frames to facilitate a quick video overview. Instead of visualizing raw videos, feature-based video visualizations focus on presenting extracted video features. Chen et al. [11] introduced visual signatures into video visual- ization and investigated different types of visual signatures through a user study. Duffy et al. [18] proposed glyph-based video visualizations that employ tadpole-like glyphs to exhibit the spatiotemporal motion features of semen for semen analysis. Storyline visualizations were also developed to understand the scenic interactions among characters for movie analysis [60, 61]. Context-based video visualizations have been established in feature-based visualizations with scene context. Romero et al. [55] introduced Viz-A-Vis, which visualizes human activities on a room map, and Botchen et al. [8] aggregated human motion trajectorieson top of representative video frames. For surveillance videos, H ¨oferlin et al. [32] developed schematic summaries aggregating trajectories on video scenes, and Meghdadi et al. [46] employed a space-time cube to layer the motion paths of moving objects on top of a video. 10 C ONCLUSION
We have presented VideoModerator, a risk-aware framework that en-
ables quick navigation and supports fast moderation of deviant videos
in e-commerce. The framework bridges the gap between human mod-
erators and machine learning models through interactive multimodal
video visualizations. First, our framework adopts a “learning with
reviewing” strategy that iteratively improves model accuracy by using
the ground-truth labels provided by users. Second, we propose an inter-
active interface that visualizes multimodal machine-generated insights
to foster an improved mixed-initiative environment. Our evaluation in-
dicates that VideoModerator can assist users in promptly understanding
video content and effectively discovering deviant clips. In the future,
we plan to deploy our system in real-world applications and evaluate it
through crowd-sourcing studies.

VIII. C ONCLUSION
We propose a new privacy-aware moderation system for
combating abusive information in EEMSs through tailored
lightweight cryptographic protocols. We ﬁrst propose a privacy-
preserving design for counting reports, threshold evaluation, and
tracing back the sources of abusive messages. We then further
propose a design for building a transparent blocklist to privately
detect forwarders’ messages, so as to mitigate the further spread
of identiﬁed abusive messages from the user-reporting-based
process. Extensive experiments demonstrate the promising per-
formance of our system.
